text: Hi everyone. Today's video is about a twenty year old man who was referred to us from our gastroenterology colleagues. He was admitted with acute severe ulcerative colitis. After failed medical management, he was considered for surgery. The patient was appropriately consented and the procedure was explained in great length. He underwent a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy with an ileostomy. Patients with severe acute ulcerative colitis have to fit in certain criteria before they are referred for a colectomy. The TrueLove and WITS is one of the most popular. There's the UC Activity Index. However, recently the British Society of Gastroenterology has published guidelines which are definitely worth reviewing for anyone interested in this topic.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic subtotal colectomy with a right hemicolectomy, identifying the ileocecal junction and grasping the peritoneum to create tension on the ileocolic pedicle. This tension aids in opening the peritoneal plane, allowing for careful dissection towards the duodenum. The medial to lateral dissection is performed, ensuring the duodenum is safely retracted. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are used to avoid tearing small veins near the pancreas, preventing bleeding. The dissection then progresses to the lateral part of the right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures, identifying the fascia of Toldt amidst edematous tissue and ascites. A small swab or gauze is used to aid dissection. After completing the medial to lateral dissection, attention shifts to the lateral dissection of congenital adhesions, extending up to the hepatic flexure. Strong traction and counter traction are applied to dissect the hepatic flexure from the inferior colic compartment, with caution taken to avoid the underlying duodenum.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic omentum in the supracolic compartment, carefully separating it from the transverse colon while preserving the blood supply to the stomach. The omentum is visibly stretched between the stomach and the transverse colon. The lesser sac is opened, and the dissection proceeds from the hepatic flexure to the splenic flexure, with the stomach and transverse colon clearly visible. Multiple benign lymph nodes are encountered during the dissection, requiring careful manipulation to avoid damage to underlying structures such as the duodenum and pancreas.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy, adjusting the operating table for optimal access. The gastrocolic omentum is fully dissected from the hepatic to the splenic flexure. The mesocolon is grasped and dissected in the supracolic compartment, staying close to the transverse colon due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon positions the patient in a steep head-down tilt with the table tilted left side up to clear the pelvis of the small bowel. The dissection proceeds inward at the level of the splenic flexure, moving from the left to the right of the patient towards the previously dissected transverse mesocolon. The greater omentum is densely adhered to the spleen, requiring meticulous dissection to avoid damaging the splenic blood supply.

During the subtotal colectomy for benign disease, the surgeon performs a left-sided dissection from lateral to medial, avoiding proximity to the origin of blood vessels to minimize risk to structures like the ureter. The dissection is taken close to the sacral promontory, and the lower sigmoid colon and upper rectum are divided using a transfer stapler.
